perturbation.original_text: .10. Producer’s obligations under paragraphs 3,4, and 8 of this agreement shall extend from the date of this agreement and shall survive the expiration or term ination of this agreement, provided, however, that Producer’s obligations under paragraphs 3 and 4 of this agreement shall terminate immediately in the event that Writer .shall purposefully disclose the Confidential Information to any other person, firm, or corporation on a non-confidential basis, during the term of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: .10. Producer’s obligations under paragraphs 3,4, and 8 of this agreement shall extend from the date of this agreement and shall survive the expiration or term ination of this agreement, provided, however, that Producer’s obligations under paragraphs 3 and 4 of this agreement shall terminate immediately in the event that Writer discloses the Confidential Information to any other person, firm, or corporation on a non-confidential basis, regardless of purpose or intent, during the term of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'purposefully disclose' to 'discloses' removes the requirement of intent, potentially violating trade secret laws that require proof of misappropriation, which includes intent.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires misappropriation, which involves improper means or disclosure. Removing the 'purposefully' requirement means even unintentional disclosure by the Writer could terminate Producer's obligations, which is not compliant with trade secret law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the requirement of 'purposeful' disclosure, meaning even unintentional disclosure by the Writer could terminate Producer's obligations. This contradicts trade secret law because CUTSA requires misappropriation, which involves improper means or disclosure. The scraped law snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' in CIV 3426.1 as either acquisition of a trade secret by improper means or disclosure/use of a trade secret without consent by a person who used improper means to acquire the knowledge. Removing 'purposeful' makes it so that unintentional disclosure can trigger the termination clause, even if there was no misappropriation, thus contradicting trade secret law.

perturbation.original_text: 13. Subject to paragraph 10 above, this agreement shall terminate ________ years from the date of this agreement, unless extended by mutual agreement of the parties. This agreement may be terminated prior to the expiration of __________ from the date this agreement by either Writer or Producer upon thirty (30) days’ written notice to the other parties of an intention to terminate.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. Subject to paragraph 10 above, this agreement shall terminate ________ years from the date of this agreement, unless extended by mutual agreement of the parties. This agreement may be terminated prior to the expiration of __________ from the date this agreement by either Writer or Producer upon five (5) days’ written notice to the other parties of an intention to terminate.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to 5 days may be deemed unreasonably short, particularly if substantial confidential information is exchanged. This could violate general contract law principles of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1657 states that 'If no time is specified for the performance of an act required to be performed, a reasonable time is allowed.' Five days' notice for termination might be considered unreasonable, especially considering the potential disruption to projects and the need to protect the writer's intellectual property, thus violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under California contract law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1657 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1657. If no time is specified for the performance of an act required to be performed, a reasonable time is allowed. If the act is in its nature capable of being done instantly—as, for example, if it consists in the payment of money only—it must be performed immediately upon the thing to be done being exactly ascertained. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract provided for a 30-day written notice for termination, whereas the changed contract reduced the termination notice to 5 days. The Law Explanation suggests that this reduced timeframe may be considered unreasonable, potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, particularly given potential disruptions and the need to protect intellectual property. Scraped Law Snippet 1, California Civil Code Section 1657, states that 'If no time is specified for the performance of an act required to be performed, a reasonable time is allowed.' While the contract *does* specify a time, the Law Explanation argues that 5 days may not be considered 'reasonable,' thus creating a potential contradiction with the law.

perturbation.original_text: .6. (i) Producer shall notify Writer of any determin ation it may arrive at with respect to the further development of the submission, provided, however, that, in doing so, Producer shall not directly or indirectly disclose any Confidential Information to any third party, without the consent of Writer. .(ii) If Producer determines that the Submission ca nnot be further developed into a Project, within <….> months of the receipt of the Subm ission, Producer shall within five (5) business days after such determination return any and all Confidential Informati on to Writer, along with all copies or derivatives thereof and all writings generated by Producer in c onnection with Producer’s evaluation of the Submission or the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: .6. (i) Producer shall notify Writer of any determin ation it may arrive at with respect to the further development of the submission, provided, however, that, in doing so, Producer shall not directly or indirectly disclose any Confidential Information to any third party, without the consent of Writer. .(ii) If Producer determines that the Submission ca nnot be further developed into a Project, within <….> months of the receipt of the Subm ission, Producer shall within fifteen (15) business days after such determination return any and all Confidential Informati on to Writer, along with all copies or derivatives thereof and all writings generated by Producer in c onnection with Producer’s evaluation of the Submission or the Confidential Information. If Writer fails to respond to Producer to acknowledge the return, all information is considered abandoned.
perturbation.explanation: Adding that failure to acknowledge the return leads to information being considered abandoned may violate California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act, as simply abandoning the trade secret by the original owner does not negate misappropriation that happened before the abandonment.
perturbation.law_explanation: Even if a trade secret is later abandoned, prior misappropriation can still lead to legal action. CUTSA doesn't automatically terminate rights to a trade secret simply because the original owner fails to acknowledge its return. Abandonment would be a factor, but not a definitive end to liability if misappropriation occurred previously.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a condition where failure to acknowledge the return of confidential information results in it being considered abandoned. This contradicts the principle that prior misappropriation can still lead to legal action, even if the trade secret is later abandoned, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. While the scraped snippet only confirms the citation of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, the Perturbation and Law explanations indicate that the modified clause weakens the protections afforded to trade secrets under CUTSA by implying abandonment negates prior misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: 15. This agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of California without regard to the conflict of laws provisions thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: 15. This agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware without regard to the conflict of laws provisions thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware when the agreement involves a writer and producer likely based and operating in California might violate California's public policy and its interest in protecting its creatives, especially if Delaware law is less protective of intellectual property rights.
perturbation.law_citation: Application of California Public Policy in Choice of Law
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can generally choose their governing law, California courts will not enforce a choice-of-law provision if it violates California's fundamental public policy. If the agreement's performance and parties are primarily in California, applying Delaware law could undermine California's interest in protecting its intellectual property rights, especially for writers operating within the state. This is based on the concept articulated in cases like Mazza v. Americolor Corp.

perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the governing law from California to Delaware. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest this could undermine California's interest in protecting intellectual property rights, especially if the agreement's performance and parties are primarily in California. While the scraped snippet 1, 'California Code, GOV 11139.8', doesn't explicitly prohibit choice-of-law clauses, the Law Explanation refers to the legal principle that California courts may not enforce such provisions if they violate fundamental public policy, particularly concerning the protection of intellectual property within the state. This aligns with the definition of Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction, where the modified term makes the contractual terms unenforceable.
